history.title: Historical Background
history.body: The State Council is the highest court of the administrative order, with both advisory and jurisdictional authority. It was created by the Constitution of the Democratic Republic of the Congo of February 18, 2006, as amended to date, following the dissolution of the former Supreme Court of Justice. Its organization, jurisdiction, and functioning are set by Organic Law No. 16/027 of October 15, 2016. The institution has been in operation since 2018.

appointment.title:
Book
Appointment
appointment.body: To arrange a meeting with the State Council, please complete the following appointment request form. Ensure all required fields are filled out accurately so we can process your request efficiently. You'll receive a confirmation email or phone call with the scheduled date and time.

appeal.title:
Appeal to the
State
Council
appeal.body1: An appeal to the State Council is an administrative legal remedy allowing a party to challenge administrative decisions and acts. The State Council verifies whether administrative law was correctly applied and whether proper procedures were followed.
appeal.body2: The State Council is seized by a request from the parties or by a declaration filed with the Council. Appeals must be filed within the prescribed legal timeframes and must be confirmed by a petition with supporting documents.

appealSteps.title: How to fill an appeal
appealSteps.subtitle: Follow these steps to submit your appeal to the State Council

1.title: Prepare your documents
1.body: Gather the contested administrative decision, your identification, and any supporting evidence. Ensure all documents are legible and properly certified where required.

2.title: Draft your petition
2.body: Write a clear petition stating the facts, grounds for appeal, and the relief you seek. Include references to applicable legal provisions.

3.title: File with the Registry
3.body: Submit your petition and supporting documents to the Registry of the State Council within the prescribed legal timeframes. Obtain a receipt as proof of filing.

4.title: Pay applicable fees
4.body: Pay any required court fees at the designated office. Keep the payment receipt for your records.

5.title: Await notification
5.body: The Registry will notify you of the case number and hearing date. You may be required to serve copies to the opposing party.

haveQuestions.title: Have questions?
haveQuestions.body: Our team is here to help you understand the appeal process.

1.question: What is the deadline for filing an appeal?
1.answer: Appeals must generally be filed within two months of notification of the contested decision. Specific timeframes may apply depending on the type of administrative act. Consult the applicable legislation or contact the Registry for guidance.

2.question: Can I represent myself or do I need a lawyer?
2.answer: You may represent yourself before the State Council. However, legal representation is recommended for complex matters. The Registry can provide information on legal aid options if you qualify.

3.question: What documents do I need to include?
3.answer: Typically you need: the contested decision, your petition, identification, and supporting documents. The Registry provides a checklist upon request. Additional documents may be required depending on your case.

4.question: How long does the appeal process take?
4.answer: Processing times vary depending on case complexity and court workload. You will receive a hearing date after your case is registered. The Registry can provide estimated timelines for similar cases.

5.question: Can I withdraw my appeal?
5.answer: Yes. You may withdraw your appeal at any time before the final decision. Submit a written withdrawal to the Registry. Note that withdrawal may have implications for any related proceedings.

offer.title: What we offer
offer.subtitle: We provide tailored legal solutions to meet your unique needs, ensuring your rights are protected and justice is served.

1.title: Supreme Judicial Authority
1.body: As the highest court of the ordinary judicial system, the State Council ensures uniform interpretation and application of law throughout the Democratic Republic of Congo.

2.title: Legal Certainty
2.body: Through our jurisprudence and decisions, we establish legal precedents that guide lower courts and ensure consistency in the application of justice.

3.title: Judicial Excellence
3.body: Our magistrates and counselors are selected based on merit, ensuring the highest standards of legal expertise, integrity, and commitment to justice.

4.title: International Cooperation
4.body: We actively participate in dialogue with international judicial bodies and maintain cooperation with French-speaking and African courts.

1.title: The first president of the State Council

paragraphs.1: As the third-ranking member of the DRC's High Council of the Judiciary, the First President of the State Council, Brigitte Nsensele Wa Nsensele, donated blood on Thursday, February 27th, in solidarity with the victims of the Rwandan aggression in the eastern part of the country.
paragraphs.2: For every democratic Congolese citizen, contributing to the war effort is a civic duty. The judiciary is no exception.
paragraphs.3: For over three decades, the DRC has suffered aggression perpetrated by Rwanda and other negative armed groups, resulting in millions of deaths, tens of thousands of displaced persons, and a deplorable humanitarian situation.

paragraphs.1: Under the leadership of the First President, the State Council has embarked on comprehensive judicial reforms aimed at modernizing the judicial system.
paragraphs.2: The reforms include the digitalization of court records, implementation of case management systems, and training programs for judges and court staff.
paragraphs.3: These initiatives reflect the Court's commitment to transparency, efficiency, and accountability in the administration of justice.

paragraphs.1: The State Council actively engages with international judicial institutions and participates in global forums to share experiences.
paragraphs.2: Through partnerships with courts in France, Belgium, and other Francophone countries, the Court ensures it remains at the forefront of legal developments.
paragraphs.3: This international engagement strengthens the DRC's judicial system and enhances its capacity to deliver justice in accordance with international standards.

hero.title: Understanding the highest jurisdiction of the administrative order in the Democratic Republic of the Congo

intro.title: Understanding the highest jurisdiction of the administrative order in the Democratic Republic of the Congo
intro.body: The highest jurisdiction of the administrative order is grounded in the Constitution of February 18, 2006 as amended to date, especially in Article 149 which provides that judicial power is assigned to courts and tribunals including, notably, the State Council. Furthermore, pursuant to Article 155 of the Constitution, Organic Law No. 16/027 of October 15, 2016 sets the organization, jurisdiction, and functioning of the administrative order jurisdictions, including the State Council. Finally, Ordinance No. 10/001 of January 10, 2019 on the internal regulations of the State Council determines the modalities for exercising the competences of this jurisdiction.

legal.title:
Access the main legal and regulatory
Texts of the State Council

docs.1: Organic Law N° 13/011-B of April 11, 2013 containing the organization, composition and functioning of the ordinary jurisdictions
docs.2: Decree N° 13/046 of September 25, 2013 rules concerning the chambers of State Council
docs.3: Decree Law N° 13/018 relative to the remuneration and allowances to the magistrates

detail.title: Organic Law N° 13/011-B of April 11, 2013 containing the organization, composition and functioning of the administrative jurisdictions
detail.paragraph1: The State Council has two main missions: that of advising administrative authorities and that of jurisdiction. In its first mission, it guides the actions of the administrative authorities of the central power as well as the organizations placed under their tutelage through its opinions, its studies and its reports.
detail.paragraph2: In its jurisdictional role, it is the supreme judge for appeals directed against the acts, regulations or decisions of the central administrative authorities or against those of public organizations placed under their tutelage as well as those of the national bodies of professional orders.

list1.2: CHAPTER II: Nomination and installation of the magistrates and clerks
list1.3: CHAPTER III: of the legal regime
list1.4: CHAPTER IV: Powers, guarantees and limits of the Magistrates

detail.paragraph5: In accordance with Article 155 of the Constitution of February 18, 2006, amended in 2011 and Articles 75 to 93 of Organic Law No. 16/027 of October 15, 2016, the State Council exercises consultative and contentious jurisdiction. Consultative competencies In consultative matters, the State Council rules, as the case may be, either by reasoned opinions. The State Council can prepare reports and make publications. Without prejudice to the provisions of the Constitution and the Organic Law on the jurisdictions of the administrative order, the State Council exercises the following consultative competencies: For the issuance of reasoned opinions, it pronounces: • On requests for legal regularity of legislative and regulatory texts under preparation or in force, introduced by the competent authorities of the central power; • On difficulties of interpretation or on questions that raise a difficulty of interpretation of applicable legal texts other than the Constitution before a jurisdiction or a central administrative authority; • On the relevance of the legal means retained by the central administrative authorities to achieve, in the most diverse areas of national life, the objectives they have set for themselves, taking into account the constraints related to administrative action; • In order to draw, at the request of the authorities of the Central Power or on its own initiative, the attention of the public authorities to the legal reforms that appear desirable for the general interest. For the drafting of service reports, it ensures: • The inspection of the jurisdictions of the administrative order in order to ascertain the material conditions of their establishment, the material and psychological environment of the work of the magistrates and the respect by the latter of the deontological rules; • The monitoring of the activities of the jurisdictions of the administrative order in order to ensure that the latter respect the legal and regulatory texts and respect the necessary unity of administrative jurisprudence; • The collection of all data or information likely to enlighten it or enlighten the public authorities for the improvement of the operating conditions of the administrative jurisdiction. For the publication of results, it ensures, by itself, by its services or by remunerated or non-remunerated third parties, research in the most diverse disciplines as well as general law, as well as public law in particular, in order to feed the administrative jurisdiction with the most recent, reliable and pointed scientific data in its disciplines. Contentious competencies Without prejudice to the provisions of the Constitution of February 18, 2006, amended in 2011, currently in force and of Organic Law No. 16/027 of October 15, 2016 relating to the jurisdictions of the administrative order, the State Council exercises the following contentious competencies: • In first and last instance, it hears appeals for annulment for violation of the law, brought against the acts, regulations or decisions of the central administrative authorities, against those of public organizations placed under their tutelage and against those of the national bodies of professional orders under the Central Power. It may also order their suspension, at the request of the parties, taking into account the factual and legal circumstances of the case. • On appeal, it hears appeals brought against the judgments rendered in this matter by the Administrative Courts of Appeal, within the scope of their competencies. It also hears on appeal appeals brought against decisions rendered by certain specialized jurisdictions of the administrative order, within the scope of their competencies, in cases where appeal is not organized internally. • On cassation, it hears appeals for violation of the Constitution, the duly ratified treaty, the law, the edict, the custom, the general principles of law and the regulation directed against the judgments and judgments of the common administrative jurisdictions or against the decisions of the specialized administrative jurisdictions. It may pronounce the cassation with or without referral. In this case, it does not rule on the merits of the dispute; except, in the event of a cassation after referral, and that the referring judge has not followed the point of law judged in cassation. • In electoral matters, it hears, on appeal, the litigation of the results of the elections of provincial deputies, governors and vice-governors of province and, on cassation, that of the elections of urban, municipal and local authorities as well as the litigation relating to the designation, by election or appointment, of members of assemblies, bodies or administrative or professional organizations or members of parties or political groupings referred to in Article 112 of Organic Law No. 16/027 of October 15, 2016. • In cases where there are no other competent jurisdictions, it hears requests for compensation relating to the reparation of exceptional, material or moral damage resulting from a measure taken or ordered by the authorities of the Central Power, the provinces and the decentralized territorial entities as well as by those of the organizations placed under their tutelage.
detail.paragraph6: The quorum required in the article is reached, when all chambers are composed as follows: the president or vice-president of chamber and two other magistrates, as well as a legal adviser and the Clerk.
detail.paragraph7: With regards to the judicial quorum in Article 89, each chamber may constitute a jurisdictional formation for the achievement of the mandate of the State Council and sit for different attributions only if the quorum is reached.
detail.paragraph8: The quorum required in the article is reached, when all chambers are composed as follows: the president or vice-president of chamber and two other magistrates, as well as a legal adviser and the Clerk. With the addition of the concerned interested party seated during the procedure with his assistance if need be and at the time of deliberations at the closed door, with participation of the Attorney General in the deliberations with participation of the clerk is done by the instruction of the matter.
detail.paragraph9: After reading and having the legal texts explained to the jurisdictions in this platform, you can request their service directly by contacting us by email or by telephone and therefore receive them.

hero.title:
Administrative Courts of
Appeal
hero.body: Organization, composition and functioning of Administrative Courts of Appeal in accordance with the organic law.

intro.title: Articles 60 to 68
intro.subtitle: Administrative Courts of Appeal: creation, composition and organization

60.title: Article 60
60.body:
One or more Administrative Courts of Appeal are established within the jurisdiction of each province as well as in the city of Kinshasa, capital of the Republic.
The territorial jurisdiction and ordinary seat of the Administrative Court of Appeal are determined by decree of the Prime Minister deliberated in the Council of Ministers.

61.title: Article 61
61.body: The Administrative Court of Appeal is composed of a First President, one or more Presidents, and Advisors.

62.title: Article 62
62.body:
In the event of absence or impediment, the First President is replaced by the most senior President, and the President by the most senior Advisor.
Seniority in rank is determined by the date and order of appointment.

63.title: Article 63
63.body: The First President or the person replacing them is responsible for service allocation.

64.title: Article 64
64.body:
The Administrative Court of Appeal comprises a consultative section and a litigation section.
Each section is subdivided into chambers.

65.title: Article 65
65.body:
Within the litigation section, cases are judged by an odd number of judges, either by one chamber or by chambers sitting jointly.
The Administrative Court of Appeal may, exceptionally, sit as a section or as sections sitting jointly for delicate or complex cases, or whenever necessary.
In such case, it is presided over by the First President.

66.title: Article 66
66.body: The chamber and the section sit respectively with at least three and five members; chambers sitting jointly and sections sitting jointly sit respectively with at least five and seven members.

67.title: Article 67
67.body:
The Plenary Assembly of the Administrative Court of Appeal includes all magistrates of that Court.
It is chaired by the First President.
It deliberates on all matters of general order concerning the entire Court or, where necessary.
The Plenary Assembly sits, as of right, in cases of reversal of jurisprudence, jurisdictional challenge, or when it must rule by judgment on a matter of principle.

68.title: Article 68
68.body:
Each Administrative Court of Appeal has a principal registrar, assisted by one or more divisional registrars, one or more registrars, as well as bailiffs.
The Administrative Court of Appeal sits with the participation of the Public Prosecutor's Office and the assistance of a registrar.

hero.title:
Administrative
Tribunals
hero.body: Organization, composition and functioning of Administrative Tribunals in accordance with the organic law.

intro.title: Articles 69 to 74
intro.subtitle: Administrative Tribunals: establishment, composition and organization

69.title: Article 69
69.body:
One or more Administrative Tribunals are established in the city of Kinshasa, in each city and in each territory.
However, a single Administrative Tribunal may be established for two or more territories.
The territorial jurisdiction and ordinary seat of Administrative Tribunals are set by decree of the Prime Minister deliberated in the Council of Ministers.

70.title: Article 70
70.body:
The Administrative Tribunal includes a consultative section and a litigation section.
Each section is subdivided into chambers.

71.title: Article 71
71.body:
The Administrative Tribunal is composed of a President, Section Presidents, Chamber Presidents and judges.
In litigation matters, the Administrative Tribunal sits with at least three judges. Exceptionally, it may sit with five judges to examine delicate or complex cases, or when necessary; in that case, the Administrative Tribunal is chaired by the head of the court.
In consultative matters, the Administrative Tribunal sits in a mixed plenary formation composed of magistrates of the section and the prosecutor's office, under the direction of the Section President.

72.title: Article 72
72.body:
In case of absence or impediment, the President is replaced by the most senior judge.
Where the number of judges of the Administrative Tribunal present at the place of hearing does not allow constitution of the bench, the President of the tribunal may appoint as an associate judge, on reasoned requisition of the Public Prosecutor, a magistrate from the prosecutor's office attached to the Administrative Tribunal, a lawyer with at least five years of seniority, or any other holder of a law degree with experience in administrative matters.
An associate judge other than the Public Prosecutor takes the following oath before the President: "I swear to respect the Constitution and laws of the Democratic Republic of Congo and to discharge faithfully and loyally, with honor and dignity, the functions entrusted to me."
The associate judge is chosen based on impartiality, notably by excluding any conflict of interest resulting from prior professional knowledge of the case file.
The President or the person replacing them is responsible for service allocation.

73.title: Article 73
73.body: Each Administrative Tribunal has a divisional registrar, assisted by one or more registrars and bailiffs.

74.title: Article 74
74.body: The Administrative Tribunal sits with the participation of the Public Prosecutor's Office and the assistance of a registrar.

hero.title:
Suggested
Reforms
hero.body: Modernizing the judicial system through strategic reforms that enhance efficiency, transparency, and access to justice for all Congolese citizens.

building.title: Building a stronger justice system
building.body: Following the 2006 Constitution, the State Council has embarked on comprehensive reforms to strengthen judicial independence, improve access to justice, and align with international standards. These initiatives focus on institutional strengthening, digital transformation, and capacity building.

initiatives.title: Strategic reform initiatives
initiatives.body: A comprehensive five-phase approach to modernizing our judicial system, ensuring excellence at every stage of transformation.

1.title: Institutional Independence
1.body: Strengthening judicial independence through constitutional safeguards and financial autonomy.

2.title: Access to Justice
2.body: Expanding legal aid services and establishing regional access points nationwide.

3.title: International Cooperation
3.body: Deepening partnerships with international judicial bodies for knowledge exchange.

4.title: Digital Transformation
4.body: Implementing modern case management systems and online jurisprudence databases.

5.title: Capacity Building
5.body: Continuous training for magistrates and advanced legal research capabilities.

hero.title:
International
Relations
hero.body: In the context of the internationalization of law and justice, the State Council of the Democratic Republic of Congo is not isolated from this environment. It actively participates in the dialogue of judges by maintaining a high level of cooperation within the framework of the Association of Supreme Courts of French-speaking countries (AHJUCAF) and the African Association of Francophone Supreme Courts (AA-HJF).

networks.title: International judicial networks
networks.subtitle: The Court maintains privileged relationships with the leading associations of Francophone and African supreme courts

ahjucaf.name: Association of Supreme Courts Using French
ahjucaf.body: A platform for exchange and cooperation between Francophone supreme courts, facilitating judicial dialogue, sharing of jurisprudence, and strengthening institutional capacities across French-speaking jurisdictions worldwide.

aahjf.name: African Association of Francophone Supreme Courts
aahjf.body: A platform for exchange and cooperation between Francophone supreme courts, facilitating judicial dialogue, sharing of jurisprudence, and strengthening institutional capacities across French-speaking jurisdictions worldwide.

domains.title: International cooperation domains
domains.subtitle: Our international engagement translates into active participation in various initiatives aimed at strengthening the quality of justice and the rule of law

dialogue.title: Judicial Dialogue
dialogue.body: Regular exchanges on jurisprudence, best practices, and common challenges faced by supreme courts, fostering mutual learning and harmonized legal interpretation.

assistance.title: Technical Assistance
assistance.body: Technical cooperation for modernization of judicial systems, digitalization, and improvement of access to justice through shared experiences.

capacity.title: Capacity Building
capacity.body: Training programs, seminars, and workshops for strengthening the skills of magistrates and judicial personnel through international expertise.

forums.title: International Forums
forums.body: Active participation in international forums to contribute to the development of international and regional law and judicial standards.

collaboration.title: Interested in Collaboration?
collaboration.body: We are open to institutional partnerships and exchanges with other national and international jurisdictions committed to strengthening justice systems.

hero.title: Hearings and roll extracts
hero.body: Access hearing schedules, roll extracts, and recent decisions.

understanding.title: Understanding audiences
understanding.body: Find guidance on how audiences are organized and how to consult recent rolls.

metrics.title: Hearing metrics
metrics.subtitle: Monthly hearing volume and outcomes over time.

recent.title: Recent role excerpts
recent.subtitle: Latest hearing excerpts
recent.empty: No role excerpts are available at this time.

assistance.title: Need Assistance?
assistance.body: Contact the State Council for help accessing documents or schedules.

firstPresident.title: The Presidency of the State Council
firstPresident.body: The First President leads the State Council, presides over its sessions, and ensures the proper functioning of the institution.

page.heroTitle: The Presidency of the State Council
page.inauguration:
The presidency of the Council of State is held by the First President. The First President has the following responsibilities: drafting the Council of State's internal regulations; allocating matters between the two sections or between the chambers; managing the registry staff assigned to the Council; managing the operating budget allocated to the administrative courts. The First President is the delegated authorizing officer.

Since February 18, 2025, Madam Brigitte Nsensele wa Nsensele has been the First President of the Council of State.
page.biographyTitle: Biography of Mrs. Brigitte Nsensele wa Nsensele
page.biography: Born in Kinshasa, Madam Brigitte Nsensele wa Nsensele is a career magistrate. She rose through all the ranks of the administrative judiciary before being appointed First President of the Council of State of the Democratic Republic of Congo in February 2025.

career.1: Magistrate at the High Court of Kinshasa
career.2: Advisor at the Council of State
career.3: Chamber President at the Council of State
career.4: Section President at the Council of State
career.5: First President of the Council of State (since February 2025)

hero.title: Organization of the State Council
hero.subtitle: Organization chart
hero.body: Introductory text about the organization of the State Council.

tiles.firstPresident: The Presidency of the State Council
tiles.juges: The Judges of the State Council

firstPresident.title: The Presidency of the State Council
firstPresident.body: The First President of the Council of State is the hierarchical authority at the top of all administrative courts. They ensure the proper functioning of the Council of State and all other administrative courts.

sectionPresidents.title: Section Presidents
sectionPresidents.body: Each of the two sections of the Council of State is headed by a President, appointed, suspended, relieved of their duties and, where applicable, replaced by decision of the First President. When they are definitively relieved of their duties by decision of the First President, former Section Presidents may, by decision of the First President and subject to availability, enjoy the same benefits as those provided for serving section presidents. The Section President distributes cases entrusted to them by the First President among the chambers, ensuring rapid and optimal processing of files, and reports to the First President.

chamberPresidents.title: Chamber Presidents
chamberPresidents.body: The chambers of each section of the Council of State, 9 in total, are headed by presidents appointed, suspended, relieved of their duties and, where applicable, replaced by decision of the First President. Each Chamber President ensures the execution of all decisions of the First President or Section President within their chamber. They may preside over a panel and conduct hearings dedicated to examining cases assigned to that panel, unless those hearings must be led by another magistrate designated as Panel President. The Chamber President ensures the independence and impartiality of magistrates in each panel.

advisors.title: Advisors to the Council of State
advisors.body: Advisors to the Council of State are assigned, reassigned and, where applicable, replaced at the chamber level by decision of the First President. They are part of the panel.

registry.title: Registry
registry.body: The Registry of the Council of State comprises a Chief Registrar, principal registrars, divisional registrars, registrars and bailiffs. They perform their duties in accordance with the laws and regulations of the Republic. The Registry is responsible for receiving and enrolling cases before the Council of State. It receives all documents from parties, whether plaintiff or defendant. It serves petitions, requisitions, briefs and other writs. Registrars and bailiffs are governed by the statute of career agents of public services. The Chief Registrar fulfills their duties with diligence and professionalism. They are responsible for maintaining order within the Registry of the Council of State, distributing tasks to registry heads and coordinating their activities.

greffe.title: Registry

cards.presidentsTitle: First Presidents of the State Council

greffe.firstPresidentsTitle: First President of the Council of State
greffe.advisorsTitle: Advisors of the Council of State

cards.presidence: The Presidency of the State Council
cards.juges: The Judges of the State Council

organization.consultativeSectionBody:
Chambers

The consultative section comprises the following three chambers:
1. The advisory chamber;
2. The chamber for interpretation of legal texts;
3. The chamber for studies and permanent inspection.

Composition

The consultative section is composed of a Section President, Chamber Presidents and Advisors.
The consultative section is presided over by President KIBWE MUTER Eugène since April 2, 2025.
organization.consultativeSectionBio: KIBWE MUTER Eugène holds a Doctorate in Law from the University of Kinshasa. In the magistracy he served as: Deputy Prosecutor at the High Court of Haut-Katanga in Kipushi (1990 – 1993); Judge at the Tribunal de Paix de Kikwit (1994 – 1996); President of the Tribunal de Paix de Feshi (1996 – 1999); Judge at the High Court of Kinshasa/N'djili (1999 – 2002); Judge at the High Court of Kinshasa/Matete (2002 – 2013); Advisor at the Court of Appeal of Kinshasa/Gombe (2013 – 2018); Advisor at the State Council (2018 – 2023). Since August 15, 2023 he is President of the State Council.
organization.litigationSectionBody:
Chambers

The litigation section comprises six (6) chambers for investigation and adjudication of the following cases:
1. The administration chamber, responsible for legality disputes and exceptional damage compensation;
2. The public finance and taxation chamber, responsible for tax, parafiscal and customs disputes as well as public finance, public procurement and public works disputes of the Central Government;
3. The social affairs chamber, responsible for career conflicts of State agents and civil servants, including disputes related to retirement, pensions, remuneration and social benefits;
4. The elections, political parties and professional organizations chamber, responsible for election disputes other than presidential and legislative elections as well as disputes related to the organization, functioning and financing of political parties and groupings or professional organizations;
5. The economic affairs chamber, responsible for settling disputes of an economic or technical nature and those related to competition;
6. The general affairs chamber, responsible for settling all matters not expressly assigned to other chambers by this organic law.

Composition

The litigation section comprises a Section President, Chamber Presidents and Advisors.
The litigation section is presided over by President MASANI MATSHI Hippolyte.
organization.litigationSectionBio: MASANI MATSHI Hippolyte holds a Doctorate in Law from the University of Kinshasa, associate professor at the Faculty of Law of the University of Kinshasa. In the magistracy he served as: Deputy Prosecutor at the High Court of Bukavu (1991 – 1995); Judge at the High Court of Uvira (1995 – 1997); Advisor at the Court of Appeal of Bandundu (1997 – 2007); President of the Court of Appeal of Kindu (2007 – 2009); First President of the Court of Appeal of Bandundu (2009 – 2011); Advisor at the Supreme Court of Justice (2011 – 2018). He is currently President of the State Council.
organization.greffeSectionBody:
The State Council maintains:
• the registry of the litigation section; and
• the registry of the consultative section.

Composition

In accordance with Article 55 of the organic law, the registry of the State Council comprises a Chief Clerk, principal clerks, divisional clerks, clerks and bailiffs. They perform their duties.

services.title: Our services
services.subtitle: Explore the divisions and offices that support the work of the State Council.

rooms.title: Rooms
rooms.body: Administrative and judicial rooms that organize deliberations and hearings.

council.title: Council
council.body: The office that coordinates policy and institutional governance.

registers.title: Registers
registers.body: Units that manage records, archives, and official filings.

chambers.title: The Chambers
chambers.body1: Each chamber handles specialized matters within its jurisdiction.
chambers.body2: Chambers sit in formations according to the applicable law.

rooms.title: Rooms and Chambers
rooms.body1: Rooms organize hearings and prepare case files.
rooms.body2: They ensure procedural compliance and scheduling.
rooms.body3: They support deliberations with administrative coordination.
rooms.body4: They ensure the smooth flow of cases to final decisions.

council.title: Council Office
council.body1: The Council Office ensures strategic coordination.
council.body2: It prepares plenary sessions and policy orientations.
council.body3: It oversees implementation of decisions and reforms.
council.body4: It supports communication and institutional relations.

registers.title: Registers and Archives
registers.body1: Registers receive and record filings and decisions.
registers.body2: They maintain archives for long-term preservation.
registers.body3: They provide certified copies upon request.
registers.body4: They manage the indexing and retrieval of case files.
registers.body5: They ensure confidentiality and integrity of records.

summary.advisor: Provides advisory support on administrative and judicial matters.
summary.nsensele: Leads judicial coordination and ensures consistent application of State Council jurisprudence across chambers.
summary.bombulu: Oversees appellate coordination and guides chamber leadership on procedural standards.
summary.ndala: Supports strategic planning and supervises policy alignment across the Court's divisions.
summary.masani: Coordinates chamber operations and drives procedural modernization initiatives.
summary.funga: Guides jurisprudential review and ensures consistency across legal interpretations.
summary.esambo: Leads training oversight and supports institutional capacity building.
summary.malenga: Oversees chamber quality assurance and jurisprudence harmonization.
summary.abdala: Coordinates outreach with legal stakeholders and ensures protocol adherence.
summary.kibwe: Supports appellate governance and strengthens cross-chamber institutional coordination.
summary.bulambo: Drives chamber-level governance and contributes to procedural consistency across matters.
summary.odioNonde: Served as First President of the State Council from 2022 to 2025 and contributed to institutional consolidation.
summary.vunduawe: Served as First President of the State Council from 2018 to 2022 and helped establish the Court in its early years.
